content.overview:
Poliomyelitis (polio) is a highly contagious infectious disease caused by the poliovirus. It primarily affects children under 5 years of age. The virus invades the nervous system and can cause irreversible paralysis within hours. There is no cure — only prevention through vaccination. India achieved polio-free status in 2014, certified by WHO, making it one of the most celebrated public health achievements in history. However, the threat of re-importation from remaining endemic countries (Pakistan, Afghanistan) makes continued vaccination critically important. Wild poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3 exist — type 2 and 3 are eradicated; only type 1 remains in 2 countries.

- Polio has been reduced by over 99% globally since the Global Polio Eradication Initiative (GPEI) launched in 1988.
- India was declared polio-free on 27 March 2014 — the last case was detected on 13 January 2011 in West Bengal.
- Wild poliovirus type 2 (WPV2) was declared eradicated in 2015; Wild poliovirus type 3 (WPV3) eradicated in 2019.
- As of 2024, wild poliovirus type 1 (WPV1) remains endemic only in Pakistan and Afghanistan.
- 1 in 200 infections leads to irreversible paralysis; among paralysed, 5–10% die from respiratory muscle paralysis.
- Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV) and Inactivated Polio Vaccine (IPV) are both safe and effective.
- Pulse Polio Programme: India's landmark immunisation programme — administering OPV to all children under 5.
- cVDPV (Circulating Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus): A rare risk when OPV is used in under-immunised populations.

Q: Is India still polio-free?
A: Yes, as of 2024, India remains polio-free. The last wild poliovirus case was on 13 January 2011. However, continued vaccination is critical to prevent re-importation from Pakistan and Afghanistan.

Q: My child already had polio drops — do they need them again during Pulse Polio?
A: Yes. All children under 5 should receive polio drops during Pulse Polio days, even if fully vaccinated. Extra doses boost immunity and protect communities. It is free and safe.

Q: Is the polio vaccine safe?
A: Yes. OPV and IPV have exceptional safety records. OPV carries a very small risk of vaccine-derived polio (VDPV) — approximately 1 per 2.7 million doses — which is why India uses both OPV and IPV together.

Q: Can polio return to India?
A: It is possible through importation from countries where polio is still endemic (Pakistan, Afghanistan). This is why sustained high vaccination coverage and AFP surveillance are essential.

Q: Are polio survivors at risk of new symptoms?
A: Post-Polio Syndrome (PPS) affects some survivors decades later with new muscle weakness and fatigue. This is not caused by new infection — it is a late effect of the original nerve damage. See a doctor for management.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
Polio is preventable — but there is no cure. Never miss a vaccination dose. Ensure all children under 5 receive OPV drops every time Pulse Polio camps are held. If you notice sudden paralysis in a child, seek emergency medical care and report to the health authorities — AFP surveillance is critical for keeping India polio-free. Treatment and vaccines are FREE at government facilities.
types: Wild Poliovirus Type 1 (WPV1): Still circulating in Pakistan and Afghanistan (as of 2024)
Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus (VDPV): Rare; occurs when OPV virus mutates in under-immunised populations
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
symptoms: Most poliovirus infections produce no symptoms (70–95%):
Non-paralytic polio (minor illness, ~24% of cases): Fever, sore throat, nausea, vomiting, headache — resolves in 1–2 weeks
Paralytic polio (~1% of all infections): Onset of flaccid (floppy) paralysis, usually in legs — typically one limb affected more than another; rapid onset within hours; may be permanent
Affects 25–40% of people who had paralytic polio, decades later
New muscle weakness, fatigue, pain in previously affected limbs
Not contagious; managed with physiotherapy and supportive care
warning_signs: Sudden onset of weakness or paralysis in any limb in a child — seek immediate medical care
Acute Flaccid Paralysis (AFP): Any child under 15 with sudden flaccid paralysis must be reported — this is a notifiable condition in India (part of AFP surveillance under NPSP)
Difficulty breathing or swallowing (bulbar polio — affects muscles of breathing)
High fever with muscle pain and sudden limb weakness
risk_factors: Living in areas with poor sanitation and unsafe water
Close contact with a person infected with poliovirus
Travel to or contact with travellers from countries where poliovirus circulates (Pakistan, Afghanistan)
Faecal-oral route (primary): Contaminated water/food with faeces of infected person
Poliovirus multiplies in the intestine and is excreted in faeces for weeks (even in asymptomatic cases)
Spreads easily in areas with poor sanitation and unsafe drinking water
prevention: Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV): Two types: bivalent OPV (bOPV — types 1 and 3) and monovalent; given as drops in mouth
Inactivated Polio Vaccine (IPV): Injected; part of routine immunisation in India
Complete immunisation with both OPV and IPV provides full protection
Wash hands with soap after using toilet and before eating/preparing food
Safe disposal of human waste (toilets, latrines)
Open defecation free (ODF) status under Swachh Bharat Mission reduces polio risk
environmental_control: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
vaccination: Birth: OPV (birth dose) + IPV at 6 weeks
6 weeks: OPV + IPV (Pentavalent vaccine contains IPV)
National Immunisation Days (NIDs): Nationwide campaigns, usually January–February
All children under 5 receive OPV drops FREE — regardless of previous vaccination status
Door-to-door polio booths set up in every community
Target: Zero-dose and under-immunised children in high-risk areas (UP, Bihar, MP — historically high-risk)
India requires travellers arriving from Pakistan and Afghanistan to show proof of polio vaccination
Travellers to polio-endemic countries should ensure they are fully vaccinated
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
diagnosis: Clinical: Acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) in child under 15 — immediately reportable
Stool specimens: Two samples collected 24–48 hours apart within 14 days of paralysis onset; sent to WHO-accredited labs
AFP Surveillance: India has nationwide AFP surveillance system under National Polio Surveillance Programme (NPSP) to detect any suspected polio case
treatment_awareness_only: NO antiviral treatment or cure for polio exists
Supportive care: Rest, pain relief, physiotherapy to maintain muscle function
Respiratory support (ventilator) for bulbar polio affecting breathing muscles
Physiotherapy and assistive devices (calipers, wheelchairs) for long-term rehabilitation
Government provides free rehabilitation support for polio survivors through ALIMCO (Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation) and state schemes
when_to_see_a_doctor: Any sudden weakness or paralysis in a child (or adult) — report immediately to the nearest health facility
High fever with muscle pain in a child followed by limb weakness
AFP (Acute Flaccid Paralysis) is a public health notifiable condition — all clinicians must report cases immediately
Post-polio syndrome symptoms in an adult who had childhood polio
india_specific_context: India celebrated 10 years of polio-free status in 2024 — a landmark in global health
Pulse Polio Programme, launched in 1995, was critical — reached hundreds of millions of children
NPSP (National Polio Surveillance Programme): WHO-supported; active AFP surveillance
High-risk areas: Western UP, Bihar, Mumbai (migrant populations) remain priority areas for surveillance
Migrant polio monitoring: Children of migrants tracked at bus/rail stations
Polio survivors: India has millions of adults with polio-related paralysis — ALIMCO provides free/subsidised callipers, crutches, and wheelchairs
IPV was introduced into India's UIP in 2015 (replacing some OPV doses) to reduce vaccine-derived risk
